agent_output:
This is absolutely NOT "just an infrastructure optimization." Let me produce a thorough domain analysis — this proposal has serious legal, contractual, and operational implications that must be surfaced before anyone writes a line of code.

```json
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days in a logistics platform operating in EU/UK markets, serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as infrastructure optimization, but delivery records are legal evidence, contractual obligations, and compliance artifacts — not mere storage.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle / Data Governance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete data artifact capturing proof of delivery, shipment history, chain of custody, timestamps, recipient confirmation, and associated metadata. Functions as a legal document, not just an operational log." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The enforceable rules governing how long specific categories of data must be preserved, driven by legal statute, contractual obligation, and business need — not storage cost." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location and time. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory compliance." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The downstream use of delivery data by B2B logistics customers for their own tax, customs, audit, and regulatory obligations. Our data retention directly constrains their compliance capability." },
      { "term": "Statutory Retention Period", "definition": "The minimum period a business is legally required to retain records under applicable law. Violating this is not a policy choice — it is a legal breach." },
      { "term": "Limitation Period", "definition": "The legal window during which a claim (contractual, tort, consumer) can be brought. Records must be retained at least through this period to mount or defend against claims." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be retained for the minimum period required by the most restrictive applicable statute across all jurisdictions of operation.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "UK HMRC requires business records retention for 6 years (VAT Act 1994, s.58). EU member states typically require 6-10 years for tax/commercial records (e.g., Germany: 10 years, France: 10 years, Netherlands: 7 years). Deleting before statutory minimums is a legal violation, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record shall be deleted before the statutory retention period of its applicable jurisdiction has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery records that serve as proof of delivery must be retained for at least the full limitation period for contractual and consumer claims in the applicable jurisdiction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "UK Limitation Act 1980 allows breach of contract claims up to 6 years. EU consumer protection directives allow claims up to 2-6 years depending on member state. Destroying evidence before the limitation period expires leaves the company unable to defend against claims.",
      "testable_assertion": "No POD record shall be deleted while a valid legal claim could still be brought against that delivery under applicable limitation law."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the period specified in the customer's service agreement or the period required for the customer's own statutory compliance obligations, whichever is longer.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "B2B customers contractually depend on access to delivery data for their own compliance reporting. Deleting their data before their obligations expire is a breach of contract and directly causes compliance failures for the customer.",
      "testable_assertion": "No B2B customer delivery record shall be deleted before the maximum of (contractual retention period, customer's statutory obligation period) has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "GDPR data minimization does not mandate short retention — it requires retention to be justified and proportionate. Legal, contractual, and legitimate business interests are valid bases for multi-year retention.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation but explicitly permits retention for as long as necessary for the purposes of processing. Tax compliance, legal defense, and contractual obligations are recognized lawful purposes. A 7-year policy already aligns with this.",
      "testable_assertion": "The data retention policy shall document a lawful basis under GDPR for each retention period, and this basis shall be reviewed annually."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Customs and cross-border shipping records must be retained per applicable customs regulations (typically 3-7 years depending on jurisdiction).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "EU Union Customs Code Article 51 requires retention of customs-relevant documents for at least 3 years. UK HMRC requires customs records for 4-6 years. Cross-border deliveries carry additional record-keeping obligations that cannot be shortened by internal policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "No customs-relevant delivery record shall be deleted before the applicable customs retention period has elapsed."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer requests delivery data from 18 months ago for a tax audit by their national authority", "expected_behavior": "Data must be available. Under the 90-day policy, it would be destroyed, causing the customer to fail their audit and triggering contractual liability for the platform." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer files a chargeback or delivery dispute 4 months after delivery", "expected_behavior": "POD and delivery records must be available to defend against the claim. Under the 90-day policy, evidence would already be destroyed." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or an EU tax authority audits the platform's VAT records 3 years after a transaction", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records supporting VAT calculations and declarations must be available. 90-day deletion would constitute destruction of legally required records." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer's contract specifies 5-year data access for their compliance needs", "expected_behavior": "The platform is contractually obligated to retain and provide access to that data. Unilateral deletion at 90 days is a breach of contract." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border shipment is investigated by customs authorities 2 years after delivery", "expected_behavior": "Customs documentation and delivery records must be producible. Failure to produce them can result in fines, penalties, and loss of customs privileges." },
    { "scenario": "Litigation is initiated against the platform 5 years after a delivery, within the limitation period", "expected_behavior": "The platform must be able to produce delivery records as evidence. Intentional pre-litigation destruction of records may constitute spoliation of evidence." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Potential regulatory fines for failure to retain statutory records (HMRC penalties, EU member state tax penalties). Inability to defend against chargebacks and disputes (direct revenue loss). Contractual breach claims from B2B customers. Potential GDPR fines are NOT reduced by this change — GDPR does not require 90-day deletion. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost B2B contract.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. B2B customers who depend on historical delivery data for compliance reporting will lose access to data they contractually expect. This will generate support escalations, contract renegotiations, and potential customer churn. Internal teams (finance, legal, compliance) will lose access to records needed for audits, dispute resolution, and reporting.",
    "user": "HIGH. B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries for returns, warranty claims, and disputes after 90 days. B2B customers face compliance failures in their own operations. Trust in the platform as a reliable data custodian is undermined.",
    "risk": "EXTREME. This change creates legal liability across multiple dimensions: statutory record-keeping violations, contractual breaches, inability to defend litigation, potential spoliation of evidence, and customs non-compliance. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect — this is a fundamental change to the platform's legal and contractual posture.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B logistics customers", "impact": "Loss of delivery data needed for their own tax, customs, and regulatory compliance. Direct breach of service agreements specifying data retention periods. May force customers to seek alternative platforms." },
      { "group": "B2C consumers", "impact": "Loss of delivery history for disputes, returns, and warranty claims. Reduced trust in platform reliability." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax team", "impact": "Inability to support VAT audits, tax filings, and financial reconciliation. Records destroyed before statutory audit windows close." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance team", "impact": "Unable to produce records for litigation defense, regulatory inquiries, or compliance audits. Potential personal liability for officers in some jurisdictions." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Unable to investigate or resolve delivery disputes older than 90 days. Increased unresolvable complaints." },
      { "group": "Platform itself (corporate entity)", "impact": "Regulatory fines, contractual liability, litigation risk, reputational damage. In extreme cases, loss of customs operator status or business licenses." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 90,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for record retention in EU/UK logistics is well-established and unambiguous. Tax retention requirements (6-10 years), limitation periods (6 years UK), and customs obligations (3-6 years) are statutory — not matters of interpretation. The 90-day proposal violates multiple hard legal requirements. The only uncertainty is in the specific contractual terms with B2B customers, which would need to be reviewed individually.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC 6-year retention requirement for VAT records",
      "EU member state statutory retention periods (6-10 years)",
      "UK Limitation Act 6-year period for contract claims",
      "Customs record retention obligations under EU Union Customs Code",
      "B2B customers' dependency on delivery data for their own compliance",
      "This change is NOT merely infrastructure — it has direct legal and business consequences"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Specific contractual retention commitments in individual B2B customer agreements (need to review contracts)",
      "Exact retention periods in each EU member state the platform operates in (varies by country)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform files VAT returns in UK and/or EU jurisdictions (standard for logistics platforms)",
      "Delivery records are used as supporting evidence for VAT and tax filings",
      "B2B customer agreements include some form of data access or retention commitment",
      "Some deliveries involve cross-border/customs-relevant shipments"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The engineering team characterizes this as 'no business logic changes' and 'infrastructure optimization.' This is factually incorrect. Changing data retention from 7 years to 90 days is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and business capabilities. This framing must be corrected before any implementation proceeds.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering team", "Legal/Compliance", "B2B customers", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive to cold storage after 90 days (keeps data, reduces cost), delete only after statutory periods expire",
        "C: Proceed with 90-day deletion only for non-statutory, non-contractual data (e.g., internal operational logs) while retaining delivery records for the full statutory period"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Tiered storage (hot → cold → archive) achieves the storage cost reduction goal without violating any legal or contractual obligation. This is the standard industry approach. Option C is acceptable as a complement. Option A is the safe default if engineering cannot implement tiered retention."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day deletion violates UK HMRC statutory retention requirement of 6 years for VAT-related business records", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion violates EU member state statutory retention requirements (6-10 years depending on jurisdiction)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion destroys proof of delivery before the 6-year limitation period for contractual claims expires, leaving the platform unable to defend against disputes and litigation", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion breaches contractual obligations to B2B customers who depend on delivery data for their own multi-year compliance reporting", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion destroys customs-relevant records before the statutory customs retention period expires", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "The proposal is incorrectly framed as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters the platform's legal compliance, contractual obligations, and dispute resolution capability", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-5" }
  ]
}
```

**Bottom line: This proposal is rejected.** Auto-deleting delivery records at 90 days would violate statutory retention laws in every EU/UK jurisdiction you operate in, breach B2B customer contracts, destroy evidence needed for dispute resolution, and expose the platform to regulatory fines, litigation, and customer churn.

The correct path to reduce storage costs is **tiered storage** (hot → cold → archive), not deletion. Move records older than 90 days to cheaper cold storage, but retain them for the full statutory period (6-10 years depending on jurisdiction). This achieves the cost reduction goal without breaking a single law or contract.

The framing of "no business logic changes" is the most dangerous part of this request — it signals that the legal and contractual implications have not been considered. Legal and compliance teams must be consulted before this goes any further.
